Part B.key_evidence: Single first detection (ndethist=1, deltajd=0) at r=19.56 with positive subtraction (isdiffpos=t). The nearest PS1 source is 9.6" away with sgscore1≈0.015 (galaxy-like) and PS1 mags ~20–21; classtar=0.973 and fwhm=2.58 pix indicate a point-like PSF. Cutouts show noisy science/reference with no obvious bright counterpart at the center in the reference, and a marginal central residual in the difference panel.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with a supernova candidate: a new, point-like, positive-subtraction source with no prior detection history at that spot, and a nearby (9.6") faint galaxy-like PS1 neighbor that could plausibly be an associated host. Magnitude (~19.6) and single-epoch appearance are consistent with an early SN detection.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A bogus/noise artifact is plausible given the very noisy cutouts and the weakness of the central residual—image differencing can produce spurious point-like residuals at this magnitude. An AGN is less likely because the nearest PS1 source is 9.6" offset (not coincident), and a Galactic variable is disfavored by the PS1 neighbor's low sgscore1; a slow solar-system object is unlikely at ndethist=1 without a clear trailed/offset morphology.
